title: Police investigate fatal northeast shooting
content:
Officers are currently on scene responding to a shooting that occurred in the parking lot of a grocery store located in the city’s north that has resulted in the death of one man.
At approximately 7:50 p.m., today, Monday, May 11, 2026, we responded to reports of  shots fired in the parking lot of the North Hill Co-op located at 520 16 Ave. N.E.
Upon police arrival, two men were found suffering from gunshot wounds and transported to hospital, both in life-threatening condition. One man has since died.
The suspects fled the area prior to police arrival and no one is in custody at this time; however, investigators believe this shooting was targeted and there is no active risk to the public.
The store was placed into lockdown to keep staff and customers safe, and the lockdown has since been lifted.
While no road closures are in effect, we are asking the public to please refrain from accessing the area as investigators continue to work and assess the scene.
As the investigation is in its early stages, no further information is available at this time.
“We fully recognize the impact of brazen, violent acts on the larger community,” says Duty Inspector Darren Smith. “Public safety is our number one priority, and Calgarians deserve to feel safe at all times in their communities. We are asking anyone who may have witnessed the shooting to contact police immediately.”
Addressing gun violence continues to be a priority for our Service. Through investigation, prevention and enforcement activities we have co-ordinated our resources to reduce victimization and enhance community safety.
Our efforts to address this issue rely on strong community support. We are asking anyone who may have witnessed the incident, or who may have dashcam or CCTV footage from the area at the time of the shooting to contact police by calling 403-266-1234. title: Police investigate fatal assault in Woodlands - Update
content:
The victim of a
fatal assault
that occurred in the southwest community of Woodlands has been identified.
On Wednesday, May 6, 2026, at approximately 5:40 a.m., officers responded to reports of a stabbing in the 2100 block of Woodview Drive S.W. It is believed an altercation occurred between the victim and an unknown individual near 24 Street and Woodview Drive S.W.
The victim was fatally injured during the assault and was declared deceased at the scene.
After completion of an autopsy conducted by the Office of the Chief Medical Examiner today, Thursday, May 7, 2026, the victim has been identified as 16-year-old Angeline BOUCHER.
Investigators believe the weapon used in the assault may have been disposed of in the area of 24 Street and Woodview Drive S.W. People are asked to contact police immediately if they happen to locate an edged weapon in garbage or recycling bins or in the surrounding area.
At this time, investigators do not believe the incident was random.
No one is in custody at this time. Investigators continue to work to determine the circumstances that led to the assault as well as collect and review CCTV footage and speak with witnesses.
Anyone with information about this incident is asked to contact police by calling 403-266-1234. Tips can also be submitted anonymously to Crime Stoppers through any of the following methods:

title: Serious-injury motorcycle collision under investigation
content:
We are investigating a motorcycle collision that occurred yesterday in northwest Calgary.
At about 8:30 p.m., on Tuesday, May 5, 2026, a man in his 20s, operating a Yamaha motorcycle, was travelling east in the 9600 block of Shriners Road N.W., when the motorcycle exited the roadway, causing the motorcyclist to be dismounted.
The motorcyclist was transported to the hospital with life-threatening injuries.
Impairment is not believed to be a factor. Speed is being investigated as a possible factor.
May is Motorcycle Safety Month in the Alberta Traffic Safety Calendar.
Tips to stay safe on the road:
Tune and check bikes before heading out for a ride
Ride in less busy areas when possible
Be mindful of road conditions
Follow the rules of the road, especially speed limits, which are designed for ideal road conditions
Helmets are mandatory for motorcyclists
Stay out of blind spots and ensure other drivers can see you

title: Man charged in break and enter and stolen property incident
content:
District 6 members have charged a man who is believed to be responsible for the theft of more than $46,000 worth of construction equipment and tools from a job site in the city’s southeast.
Last week, officers responded to reports of a break and enter with property taken from a job site located at 6127 Barlow Trail S.E. The break-in occurred between 2:30 p.m., Thursday, April 23, 2026, and 7 a.m., Friday, April 24, 2026. It is believed an unknown man had caused extensive damage inside the job site, including tampering with CCTV equipment and spray-painting graffiti before stealing tools and fleeing the area in a truck that is believed to have been stolen from the same site.
After reviewing CCTV footage from neighbouring businesses, two men were seen transporting the stolen items north of 61 Avenue S.E. While canvassing the area, officers located a large bin and a Dewalt 60V brushless snowblower that had been hidden in trees nearby. The items were returned to the rightful owners.
Police continued their search and located the stolen truck at 5910 50 Ave. S.E., near an encampment area.
On Tuesday, April 28, 2026, at approximately 1:30 p.m., officers encountered an unknown man near the recovered stolen vehicle who appeared to match the description of one of the suspects. He was also in possession of a large knife and was arrested without incident.
As a result, Vaughn Gregory WINNICHUK, 42, is charged with one count each of possession of stolen property under $5,000 and failure to comply with a release order.
WINNICHUK is scheduled to next appear in court on Wednesday, May 6, 2026.
“Break and enters at job sites can have a significant impact on businesses and workers, often resulting in lost revenue, missing equipment and delays in important work,” says Staff Sergeant Joe Hebert of the Calgary Police Service District 6 General Investigations Unit. “We are grateful for the assistance of the local business community in helping us advance this investigation that resulted in an arrest and property returned to rightful owners.”
The investigation is ongoing and police continue to search for the second suspect.

title: Charges laid in ongoing locker theft investigations
content:
We have charged a woman in connection with a series of gym locker thefts at recreation facilities across Calgary.
In March 2026, we received reports of multiple thefts from gym lockers at City of Calgary recreation centres. In each incident, victims reported that wallets, bank cards and identification documents were stolen.
Through our investigation, we identified a suspect in two thefts that occurred at the Southland Leisure Centre. These incidents, reported by separate victims, occurred on Friday, Jan. 30, 2026, and Sunday, Feb. 15, 2026.
On Wednesday, April 15, 2026, with the assistance of the CPS Prolific Offender Engagement Team and the High River RCMP, we executed a search warrant at a residence in High River, Alta. During the search, we located stolen identification documents.
As a result, Nicole Kathryn KISER, 37, of High River, has been charged with four counts of possession of a stolen credit card and four counts of theft under $5,000. She will next appear in court on Monday, May 4, 2026.
Multiple investigations into additional gym locker thefts across Calgary remain ongoing. Since November 2025, we have received reports of more than 60 incidents at various locations. While conducting followup visits, investigators also identified additional victims who had not previously reported the thefts.
“Reporting these incidents is crucial. It allows us to investigate each occurrence, identify trends and, in some cases, link suspects to multiple incidents,” says Calgary Police Service District 1 Acting Insp. Paul Ralstin. “While these thefts may appear minor, they have real impacts on victims and their feelings of safety. We are committed to thoroughly investigating these occurrences.”
Locker theft prevention tips:
Always ensure your locker is fully locked, even if you are stepping away for a short time
Avoid bringing valuables to the gym, particularly debit and credit cards
If you must bring valuables, use a high-quality lock that cannot be easily manipulated or forced open
Be aware of your surroundings when entering locker codes
Do not allow other to follow you into gym facilities; ensure they have signed in
Ask staff about valuables lockers or staff-held storage options
Report any suspicious behaviour to gym staff
Report thefts to both your gym and police
Anyone with information about these incidents is asked to contact police by calling 403-266-1234. Tips can also be submitted anonymously to Crime Stoppers through any of the following methods:

title: Man charged in deaths of two children
content:
Clarification: in yesterday’s statement, the residential block where the vehicle was located was incorrectly identified as the 4300 block of 14 Street N.W. The correct location is the 4500 block of 14 Street N.W. This information has been corrected in both the release below and the
previous statement
.
We have charged a 37-year-old man with two counts of first-degree murder in relation to the deaths of two children found deceased in northwest Calgary yesterday.
Just after 10 a.m., on Thursday, April 30, 2026, we received a phone call that led us to the 4500 block of 14 Street N.W. Upon arrival, we located a vehicle with two deceased children inside.
The deaths were considered suspicious and our Homicide Unit began an investigation. One man was taken into custody at the scene.
The two victims have been identified as a five-year-old boy and a three-year-old girl.
We have charged the children’s father with two counts of first-degree murder. He has been remanded into custody and is scheduled to appear in court on Monday, May 4, 2026.
There is a court-imposed publication ban on identifying information of the victims and witnesses. As such, the names of the children and the accused cannot be released at this time.
“This is a devastating and unimaginable loss. Our hearts are with the victims’ loved ones, and with the officers and first responders who both attended the scene and who are seeing through the investigation,” says Staff Sgt. Mark Rahn of the Calgary Police Service Homicide Unit. “These are extraordinarily difficult investigations, both professionally and personally, and we are committed to getting justice for these victims.”
We understand this incident is deeply upsetting and will greatly impact the families and loved ones of the children, along with our entire community. Our Victim Assistance Support Team (VAST) is a free service offered to victims and witnesses of crime or tragedy.
If you or someone you know is in need of support, VAST is here to help:
https://www.calgarypolice.ca/about-us/our-people/victim-assistance-support-team.html

YouthLink Calgary Police Interpretive Centre to unveil new name at 30th anniversary celebration
The following release is being shared on behalf of the YouthLink Calgary Police Interpretive Centre.
YouthLink Calgary Police Interpretive Centre will mark its 30th anniversary on Wednesday, April 29, 2026, with the public unveiling of a new organizational name, signaling an expanded vision for community safety, education and public engagement.
The new name will be revealed during a milestone evening celebration attended by Alberta Deputy Premier and Minister of Public Safety and Emergency Services Mike Ellis, Calgary Mayor Jeromy Farkas and Calgary Police Service Chief Katie McLellan.
The event will also feature the launch of a new Cyber Safety Exhibit, designed to help young people, families and educators navigate today’s digital risks. There will also be a major provincial funding announcement to support YouthLink Calgary’s crime prevention initiatives.
About YouthLink Calgary Police Interpretive Centre:
YouthLink Calgary Police Interpretive Centre is a non-profit interactive learning centre dedicated to crime prevention and public education. Over the past 30 years, YouthLink Calgary has engaged more than 500,000 youth and community members through immersive, hands-on programming on topics such as cyber safety, drugs, gangs, healthy relationships, bullying and community policing.

title: Charges laid for sexual assault
content:
We have charged a man with sexual assault following a lengthy investigation that spanned nearly three years.
On Sunday, July 23, 2023, between 1 and 2 a.m., a woman was walking in the 1200 block of Fourth Street S.W. when she was approached by a man who offered her money in exchange for sex.
She declined, after which the man sexually assaulted her. The suspect then ran away.  The woman attended the Sheldon Chumir Health Centre for medical treatment, at which point police were contacted. Officers viewed CCTV footage from the area but were unable to identify the suspect. However, they did seize biological evidence which was ultimately used to create a DNA profile which, at the time, did not match any existing profiles in police databases.
Nearly a year-and-half later, on Monday, Dec. 9, 2024, shortly before 3 a.m., another woman was sleeping outside in the 1200 block of 17 Avenue S.W, when a man offered her money in exchange for sex. After she refused, the man sexually assaulted her, then fled the area.
The victim called 911 and was taken to the hospital, where biological evidence was once again collected for forensic identification.
DNA analysis is a methodical and lengthy process. While that work continued in the laboratory, officers continued their investigation on the street.
After viewing CCTV footage from the area of the second assault, and gathering other evidence found on the scene, police identified a suspect.
Officers obtained a warrant to secure a DNA sample from that suspect, which was secured, then sent for analysis.
On Tuesday, April 14, 2026, laboratory results matched the DNA from the two previous sexual assaults with that obtained from the suspect.
The next day, Wednesday, April 15, 2026, police arrested Gramey WELDESELSA, 37, of Calgary.
WELDESELSA is now charged with two counts of sexual assault and will be in court on Wednesday, May 27, 2026.
Police believe there are other victims and are encouraging anyone with any information to contact them.
A photo of WELDESELSA is available on The City of Calgary Newsroom.
In Canada, there is no time limit on reporting a sexual assault. Anyone who believes they are a victim of sexual assault is encouraged to report it, even if many years have passed since the incident. Learn more about consent and reporting sexual assault: www.calgary.ca/sexualassault.
Anyone with information about this incident is asked to contact police by calling 403-266-1234. Tips can also be submitted anonymously to Crime Stoppers through any of the following methods:

title: Charges laid in bank impersonation frauds
content:
We have laid charges in an organized
fraud scheme
that resulted in the loss of approximately $1.3 million from Calgarians.
The scam involved offenders using technology to impersonate a victim’s financial institution over the phone. While posing as representatives of the victim’s bank, the scammers informed victims that unauthorized transactions had occurred and the victim’s accounts had been compromised. In many cases, the offenders possessed detailed personal and financial information about the victims.
During their interactions, victims were manipulated into providing sensitive banking credentials, including online logins and debit card PINs. After obtaining this information, victims were instructed to place their bank cards in an envelope for courier collection. Couriers retrieved the envelopes and delivered the cards to the offenders, who then withdrew large amounts of cash, and made purchases including gift cards and high-end items.
In 2025, we received more than 80 reports consistent with this scam, representing an estimated combined financial loss of approximately $1.3 million.
Our Financial Crimes Investigation Unit identified 15 of the impersonation scam files that were believed to be connected to the same group, which had occurred between March and August 2025.
After a thorough investigation, which included over 80 production orders and the review of a significant amount of physical and digital evidence, on Tuesday, April 14, 2026, we executed a search warrant on a residence in the 4700 block of Dalton Drive N.W. Two men were arrested and the search resulted in the seizure of:
More than $59,000 in cash
Multiple debit, credit and identification cards
A small amount of cocaine
On Wednesday, April 15, 2026, investigators also executed a search warrant on a residence located in the 14600 block of 52 Street N.W., in Edmonton, Alta., where a third man was arrested.
Sirak HABTOM, 22, of Ottawa, Ogyeth MAYUM, 22, of Ottawa and Brian-Shodu KINYAMBA, 25, of Edmonton, are charged with one count each of:
Fraud over $5,000
Unauthorized use of a credit card
Money laundering
Theft over $5,000
At the time of his arrest, MAYUM was also wanted on 10 Canada-wide warrants for weapons offences in Ontario.
HABTOM will appear in court on Friday, April 24, 2026. KINYAMBA will appear in court on Thursday, May 7, 2026. MAYUM will appear in court on Thursday, May 14.
Investigators are continuing to review evidence in the case and additional charges may be laid.
“
“This investigation points to a far more extensive fraud operation and intelligence suggests this network extends well beyond Calgary. Fraud investigations are complex and resource-intensive, with most investigations involving large volumes of digital and financial data, multiple jurisdictions and offenders who deliberately use technology to exploit public trust. Despite these challenges, our investigators remain committed to disrupting organized fraud networks and continuing to warn the public about these evolving scams.”
Staff Sgt. Peter Duchnij of the CPS Financial Crimes Investigation Unit
„
Investigators would like to thank members of the Edmonton Police Service, Ontario Provincial Police, Ottawa Police Service and Peel Regional Police Service for their support throughout this investigation.

How to protect yourself
Calgarians are reminded of the following tips to
protect themselves
from financial scams:
Be suspicious of unexpected and unusual phone calls, especially ones demanding some form of urgent action, financial or otherwise.
Do not share personal details, financial information or send money to the unknown caller.
Our officers or banks will never ask you to transfer money, cryptocurrency or gift cards as part of an investigation or suggest transactions as a way to guard your finances.
If you are suspicious of a call regarding your finances, compromised cards or an unsolicited courier service, contact your bank or financial institution using a verified number on their website or app, or visit a branch in-person.

title: Update – Police lay charges in Bridlewood arson investigation
content:
Our Arson Unit has charged a man in relation to an arson involving a home and detached garage in Bridlewood earlier this month.
At approximately 3 a.m. on Wednesday, April 1, 2026, we were called for reports of a fire involving a detached garage at a residence in the 100 block of Bridlewood Avenue S.W. Upon further investigation, our Arson Unit determined that two individuals entered the rear yard of the residence, deliberately set fire to the garage and attempted to set fire to the home before fleeing the area in a waiting vehicle.
As the investigation progressed, officers determined that the suspect vehicle involved was stolen. The District 6 operations team located the suspect with the stolen vehicle and arrested the suspect.
Investigators seized multiple items from the vehicle, including a shotgun, fentanyl and a hatchet. Clothing believed to be consistent with what was worn by one of the suspects at the time of the arson was also recovered.
As a result, Michael Keith CASELEY, 49, has been charged with:
Arson with disregard for human life
Possession of drugs
Possession of stolen property over $5,000
Possession of stolen property under $5,000
Driving while disqualified
Traffic offenses
CASELEY is facing additional charges pending examination of the firearm found in the vehicle at the time he was arrested. He is scheduled to next appear in court on Wednesday, April 22, 2026.

title: Two additional men charged in March extortion shooting
content:
Earlier this month we announced charges in relation to a series of extortion shootings that took place in February and March. Last week, two additional men were charged.
On Sunday, Feb. 22, 2026, at around 2:45 a.m., police were called for reports of a shooting outside of a residence in the 8400 block of Saddleridge Drive N.E. It is believed that two suspects fired multiple rounds into the residence from a vehicle before fleeing the scene prior to police arrival.
On Wednesday, March 4, 2026, at around 5:10 a.m., the same residence was once again targeted, and shots were fired toward the residence by three suspects inside a vehicle.
After identifying a possible suspect vehicle during a traffic stop on Saturday, March 7, 2026, investigators analyzed evidence from the stop alongside information from earlier incidents. This work helped establish links between suspects, vehicles and a series of extortion-related shootings.
On Friday, April 3, 2026,
police laid charges against one individual
in relation to the investigation while continuing to pursue the additional suspects.
Last week, investigators arrested a second man in Edmonton and issued warrants for a third.
Karanbir SINGH, 21, has been charged with one count of discharging a firearm with intent. He was remanded into custody and will appear in court today, Monday, April 20, 2026.
Germanjeet SINGH, 22, is wanted on a warrant for discharging a firearm with intent. He is described as 5’8” tall, 198 pounds, with brown eyes and black hair. Photos of him is available on The City of Calgary Newsroom.

title: Seeking public assistance in shooting investigation
content:
We are looking for information from the public after two men attended a northeast Calgary hospital with gunshot wounds last month.
Just after 3 a.m., on Saturday, March 28, 2026, two men attended the Peter Lougheed Centre, located at 3500 26 Ave. N.E., suffering from gunshot wounds. Patrol officers circulated multiple areas attempting to locate the scene of the shooting, however, were unsuccessful. No shootings were reported to police.
The victims have since been released from hospital.
Investigators have been able to determine that the victims had attended an event at the Empire Banquet Hall, located at 4826 11 St. N.E., earlier that night. After a review of CCTV, it is believed the victims left the banquet hall just after midnight and it remains unknown where or when the shooting took place.
At this stage, we believe the shooting was likely targeted, however we are looking for anyone with information about this incident, including where the shooting occurred, to come forward.
“The presence of firearms in our community is a significant concern. Targeted or not, shootings put our entire community at risk,” says Det. Kim Zinn of the CPS District 4 General Investigations Unit. “We are asking anyone with information to contact police. Sometimes even the smallest piece of information can be the puzzle piece we need.”
Our efforts to address gun violence rely on strong community support. If you have information that could help an investigation, such as witnessing suspicious behaviour or vehicles, please contact police.

title: Weapon incident at Mount Royal University resolved
content:
We have safely resolved an incident that occurred this afternoon at Mount Royal University.
At approximately 2:45 p.m., today, Monday, April 13, 2026, we responded to a report of an individual carrying a large knife at Mount Royal University (MRU), located at 4825 Mount Royal Gate S.W. Police contained the area and after confirming the witness reports, a shelter in place order was issued for the university and surrounding communities.
While in the process of clearing the scene, officers received information that the individual was a staff member working as a chef for the university. After verifying this information, the shelter in place was lifted.
“This incident highlights how seriously we treat reports of potential threats. Our officers responded quickly to ensure the safety of the campus and surrounding community,” says Insp. Darren Smith of the CPS Real Time Operations Centre. “Although we now know the public was not in danger, our officers are well trained for these situations.
This investigation ongoing and charges may be laid. One man was taken into custody from the scene.
We appreciate the cooperation of Mount Royal University staff and the public while the situation was resolved.
Statement from Mount Royal University:
“We appreciate the prompt response from the CPS as we worked closely with them throughout their response. We understand the stress this incident has caused our students and employees and are grateful for this positive outcome.”

title: Charges laid in break and enter series targeting Calgary businesses
content:
We have charged two men with 67 criminal offences and recovered firearms, drugs and stolen property in relation to a break and enter series that occurred earlier this year.
Between Saturday, Jan. 3, 2026, and Wednesday, Jan. 21, 2026, seven commercial break and enters occurred throughout Calgary at gaming, electronics and liquidation stores. During the incidents offenders smashed the front of the stores, causing significant damage, and stole various items, including gaming cards, electronics and knives. The total dollar loss between the break and enters was estimated to be $30,000, which includes the stolen items and property damages.
Investigators from the District 4 Break and Enter Team were able to link the break and enters to the same suspects, and on Wednesday, Feb. 11, 2026, officers executed a search warrant on a residence located in the 1400 block of 42 Street N.E.
As a result of the search, police seized:
A large quantity of gaming cards
Three firearms, including a prohibited handgun, a sawed-off shotgun and a rifle
An extended magazine
A taser
$35,000 worth of drugs, including fentanyl, methamphetamine, cocaine and psilocybin
Three stolen vehicles
T
hree stolen bicycles
A stolen licence plate
31 stolen government issued identification documents
Multiple vehicle keys and devices used to duplicate key fobs
Devices used to create fraudulent documents
Two men have since been arrested and charged.
Chad SNIDER, 38, of Calgary, is charged with 49 offences, including hazardous storage of a firearm, possession of a restricted weapon, possession of a restricted weapon with readily available ammunition, unauthorized possession of a firearm, possession of a controlled substance for the purpose of trafficking, possession of stolen property, break and enter, disguise with intent, possession of identity documents and failing to comply with conditions of a release order. SNIDER will next appear in court on Friday, April 17, 2026.
Coady Philip MACINTYRE, 37, of Calgary, is charged with 18 offences including break and enter, disguise with intent and failing to comply with conditions of an undertaking. MACINTYRE will next appear in court on Monday, April 13, 2026.
“This was a concerning series of break and enters that caused substantial loss and disruption to seven Calgary businesses,” says S/Sgt. Keith Shufelt of the CPS District 4 General Investigations Unit. “Through diligent investigative work, our officers were able to hold those responsible accountable, while recovering stolen property and removing dangerous weapons from the street.”

title: Charge laid after February altercation
content:
One man has been charged after an altercation outside of a place of worship in February.
On Saturday, Feb. 7, 2026, at around 6 p.m., a religious event was being hosted at a place of worship, located in the 400 block of Meridian Road S.E. Ahead of the scheduled event, approximately 12 people arrived to protest the gathering in light of recent geopolitical events. It is believed that people associated with the centre then exited the building and verbally threatened the protestors before another man driving an SUV steered toward the group of protestors, striking one person.
The victim suffered minor injuries as a result of being struck by the vehicle.
Due to the intersection of political and religious elements, our Hate Crime Prevention Team launched an investigation into the incident.
After a thorough investigation including witness accounts and CCTV footage, one person has been charged.
Payman ESMAEILI, 49, of Calgary, has been charged with one count of assault with a weapon.
He will next appear in court on Friday, May 22, 2026.
Whether hate motivation was a factor in the altercation remains under investigation.
“We recognize that this incident is closely connected to deeply felt political and human rights tensions internationally, and that members of our community hold strong and differing views,” says Calgary Police Service acting Supt. Cory Ma. “Our role is not to take sides in political disputes, however when the incident involves threats, violence or intimidation, it is our responsibility to conduct a fair and objective investigation.”

Calgary Police Service member charged in breach of privacy investigation
Following an investigation by the Calgary Police Service Sensitive Investigations Unit, a civilian member of the Service has been charged for intentionally accessing police databases for non-police matters.
In December 2025, police were made aware of allegations that protected information had been accessed. It is believed a member of the Calgary Police Service was accessing police databases against policy and authorized use to seek information on individuals she has had, or sought to have, personal relationships with. An investigation began and investigators identified a woman who was believed to be leveraging her access to police data and facilities between Feb. 1 and Aug. 1, 2025, to retrieve protected information.
On Wednesday, March 4, 2026, a search warrant was executed, and the woman’s mobile device was seized. The investigation identified four victims who had their data targeted and intentionally searched. The impacted individuals have been notified.
“We recognize that a breach of this nature shakes the foundation of trust that we have built with the public and our members. Allegations such as these are taken very seriously, and we are committed to addressing this matter in a thorough and appropriate manner,” says Insp. Dehl Vella of the Calgary Police Service Professional Standards Section.
On Tuesday, April 7, 2026, Kayla JESSEN was arrested and charged with nine counts under the Protection of Privacy Act section 60(1)(a) for collecting, using or disclosing information in contravention of Part 1 of the Act and section 60(1)(b) gaining or attempting to gain access to personal information in contravention of the Act.
JESSEN is a three-year employee of the Calgary Police Service and is currently on an unrelated leave from the Service pending review.
She was released from custody and will next appear in court on Tuesday, June 2, 2026.
Anyone with information regarding this incident is asked to contact police by calling 403-266-1234.

title: Information sought after $43,000 spending spree using stolen wallet
content:

The suspect is described as a man in his late 20s to early 30s, about 5’11” to 6’ tall, with a medium build and a short beard. At the time of the incident, he was wearing a black hooded jacket, grey Nike sweatpants, white shoes and a black baseball cap.

title: 23 charges laid in stolen-merchandise trafficking investigation
content:
A man has been charged following an investigation into the trafficking and sale of stolen high-end merchandise from a Calgary retail store.
On Tuesday, March 10, 2026, Lululemon security notified police that an individual was selling stolen merchandise on Facebook Marketplace for roughly half the original price.
As the investigation progressed, officers identified the individual and executed a search warrant at his residence on Saturday, March 28, 2026. During the search, officers seized new Lululemon clothing, a significant number of forged identity documents and 17 pieces of stolen mail.
As a result, Tyler Anthony CHASSE, 23, of Calgary, is charged with 23 offences, including:
·
One count of possession of stolen property
·
One count of trafficking stolen property
·
One count of possession of proceeds of crime
·
Two counts of possession of stolen mail
·
18 counts of possession of forged documents
CHASSE is scheduled to next appear in court on Friday, April 10, 2026.
Retail crime may appear victimless, but it has real consequences for business owners and affects the overall sense of safety in our community. Fraud can also carry significant emotional and financial impacts, and no one should feel embarrassed or alone in coming forward.
Anyone who believes they may be a victim of fraud is encouraged to report it to the Calgary Police Service or Crime Stoppers.
Anyone with information about this incident is asked to contact police by calling 403-266-1234. Tips can also be submitted anonymously to Crime Stoppers through any of the following methods:

title: Charges laid in copper theft from medical supply business
content:
We have charged one man following a copper pipe theft that caused significant damage to a medical supply facility last summer.
On Thursday, July 24, 2025, a medical supply business located in the 400 block of 19 Street S.E., contacted police to report a break and enter at its production facility, where temperature-sensitive, lifesaving dialysis materials were stored. It is believed that a man entered the site multiple times over the course of a week, stealing custom vehicle rims and copper piping from refrigeration units used to cool the company’s warehouse. The theft caused the refrigeration units to fail, resulting in approximately $400,000 in damages.
Following a media report about the incident, a member of the public came forward with information. After an extensive investigation, officers were able to identify and locate an offender.
Peter Roy THEDORFF, 52, of Calgary, has been charged with three counts of break and enter and one count of mischief. THEDORFF will next appear in court on Thursday, April 30, 2026.
“
The damage extended well beyond the theft itself, disrupting the storage of lifesaving medical products and creating real impacts in our community. We are grateful to the member of public who came forward with information to help investigators hold this individual accountable.

Encampment removed following joint safety operation in Foothills County
A collaborative safety operation involving the Calgary Police Service (CPS), Okotoks Royal Canadian Mounted Police (RCMP) and Foothills County Peace Officers has resulted in the removal of an encampment located just outside Calgary city limits, following ongoing concerns about violence, property crime and public safety in the surrounding area.
The encampment, situated about one kilometre outside of Calgary on private rural land near 210 Avenue and 64 Street S.W., had generated numerous calls for service between Thursday, Jan. 1 and Sunday, March 1, 2026, including reports of unsafe behaviour, suspicious persons and thefts. RCMP and Foothills County Peace Officers also responded to reports of individuals from the encampment who had chased or struck passing vehicles and, in one case, threatened a road maintenance worker with a knife.
On Monday, March 23, 2026, officers from the CPS District 8 Community Engagement Response Team (CERT), Okotoks RCMP and Foothills County Peace Officers attended the site to address safety concerns and support cleanup efforts. One individual was located within a structure and arrested on outstanding warrants and trespassing offences. Officers offered support services, including transportation to Calgary to access those resources.
On Tuesday, March 24, 2026, officers returned to ensure the safety of cleanup crews and again located the same individual within the encampment. The individual was arrested for breaching release conditions. Despite multiple attempts by partner clinicians to engage, the individual declined available supports.
During the cleanup, crews removed four trailer loads of debris and items, including makeshift structures and assorted property such as two bicycles and several small household items. Four knives were also seized, and no other weapons were located.
“While we lead with compassion and prioritize connecting people to community resources, public safety remains our priority,” says CPS District 8 Staff Sgt. Graeme Smiley. “Violence and unsafe behaviour will not be tolerated, and we are grateful to our partner agencies for their commitment to working together to keep our communities safe.”
Together with our partners, we will continue concentrated efforts to enhance public safety in locations experiencing elevated levels of social disorder, criminal activity and unsafe behaviour. Our goal is to strengthen our city together for a Safer Calgary.

title: Police investigate serious-injury collision on Crowchild Trail
content:
Our Traffic Section continues to investigate after a serious-injury collision left a woman in her 40s in life-threatening condition earlier this morning.
At approximately 6:40 a.m., today, Monday, April 6, 2026, a two-vehicle collision occurred in the southbound lanes of Crowchild Trail near University Drive N.W.
At that time, a white 2024 Toyota Corolla, driven by a man in his 30s, with a woman in her 40s as a rear passenger, was travelling southbound on Crowchild Trail N.W. It is believed the Corolla collided with the rear of a 2010 Toyota Yaris, driven by a woman also in her 40s.
Investigators believe the Corolla went through a traffic barrier and left the roadway and dropped down onto the northbound lanes of Crowchild Trail N.W.
As a result of the collision, the passenger in the Corolla sustained life-altering injuries and was transported to hospital in life-threatening condition. The driver of the Corolla sustained serious injuries and was also transported to hospital. The driver of the Yaris was uninjured.
At this time, there is no evidence of alcohol or drugs being a factor in this collision.
Police are investigating if speed was a factor in relation to the adverse weather conditions present at the time of the collision.

title: Police warn Calgarians of paving scams
content:
We are warning members of the public of an ongoing scam involving paving contractors in Calgary and the surrounding area.
These contractors will approach home and business owners and claim to have just finished another paving project in the area.
They will offer to pave the customer's property with free material and only charge for labour. Home or business owners are presented with a contract showing blank spaces for the cost per square foot and area covered by the project. When the customer signs the incomplete contract, the contractor paves a large area with a very thin layer of poor quality asphalt and then completes the square footage and price on the already signed contract.
In some cases, contractors attempt to intimidate customers into paying immediately for the work , and provide false addresses and contact information.
Calgary Police advise people to research companies through the Better Business Bureau, Consumer Investigations Unit, and customer reviews before accepting any paving offers, and to contact police if contractors make threats against any person’s safety.
Anyone with information about these scams is asked to contact police by calling 403-266-1234. Tips can also be submitted anonymously to Crime Stoppers through any of the following methods:

title: Calgary Police Service expands Safer Calgary – Operation Order
content:
Yesterday, Wednesday, May 13, 2026, we continued Safer Calgary – Operation Order through more strategic patrols, focused enforcement, community outreach and referrals to support services.
Yesterday’s operation expanded beyond the downtown core to include the areas around the Chinook and Sunalta CTrain stations, and the 17 Avenue (International Avenue) S.E. corridor.
“I continue to hear how people do not feel safe in certain parts of Calgary. We cannot have this,” says Chief Katie McLellan. “We are here and we will be back to continue to protect and serve our community. Social disorder and criminal behaviour will not define this city. We will do our job, with our partners, to keep Calgary safe.”
As with other focused operations, we worked closely with our partners in Calgary Community Standards, Calgary Transit Public Safety and the Alberta Sheriffs to engage with people in the identified areas, connect them with supports and enforce laws and bylaws around drug use, possession, trafficking, social disorder and violent offences.
“Every Calgarian deserves to feel safe in their community and in the public spaces they rely on every day,” says Mike Ellis, Minister of Public Safety and Emergency Services. “This operation shows the impact of strong collaboration between the Calgary Police Service and its partners, including Alberta Sheriffs, transit peace officers and social service agencies. By combining targeted enforcement with connection to supports, this coordinated effort is helping to address crime and social disorder, while reinforcing that Calgary’s public spaces are safe, respectful and welcoming for everyone.”
"Public safety, especially in our downtown, is top of mind for many Calgarians. We must work with our partners to restore confidence in our downtown, while ensuring we are compassionate to those vulnerable Calgarians who need support," says Calgary Mayor Jeromy Farkas. "I am proud of our Calgary Police Service, and the leadership of Chief McLellan, who understands the need to target both crime and, more importantly, the root causes of crime."
Quick stats
The numbers below reflect overall totals from the operation, and are inclusive of enforcement actions from the Calgary Police Service and our partner agencies.
Calls attended: 421
Persons charged: 10
Charges laid: 33
Four charges were for possession of drugs
Four charges were weapons related
16 charges were for fail to comply with an undertaking, release order or breaches
The remainder were for a variety of offences including: assault against a peace officer, obstruction, possession of identity documents, possession of property obtained by crime, and theft.
Summonses issued: 327
Warrants executed: 308
Proactive community engagements: 497
Social support referrals: 215
Operation highlights
On Stephen Avenue, one of our officers connected with a newcomer to Calgary seeking information on local supports. The officer provided guidance, directions and shared information about services available to Calgarians.
We located a woman passed out in her vehicle in the middle of downtown rush hour traffic. The woman appeared to be on drugs and EMS was called to check on her well-being. She was arrested and will be charged with impaired driving.
A man was taken into custody on suspicion of drug trafficking near the Drop-In Centre. Upon searching him, officers located brass knuckles. He is being charged with possession of a prohibited weapon.
Within minutes of each other, officers located two men leaving the Real Canadian Superstore in the East Village with stolen items. Officers engaged with store security and recovered the stolen items.
Transit Public Safety officers located three individuals they have previously interacted with, who were found to have a total of 53 outstanding warrants related to disorder offences on transit property. They were arrested and referred to Community Court.
Calgary Transit officers in the core located a suspect we were looking for in relation to multiple assaults at a shopping mall. Transit officers located the suspect walking into traffic on Macleod Trail and detained him. He has been transferred to CPS custody and is facing additional charges as well as outstanding warrants.
While patrolling, Calgary Transit officers came across a young female who was using drugs near the City Hall CTrain Station. During their interaction, officers learned she was the victim of human trafficking. Bylaw officers offered her resources and supports and referred her to Calgary Community Standards’ outreach team. We have also referred this to the ALERT Human Trafficking & Counter Exploitation Unit for further resources and support.
Calgary Transit peace officers patrolling the Centre Street CTrain Station observed a woman openly using drugs on the platform. The woman is known to officers and was wanted on eight outstanding criminal and bylaw warrants. She was taken into custody and released on an appearance order to attend Community Court.

title: Man charged in overdose death investigation
content:
In January 2026, a man was found deceased in his residence in the community of Coral Springs resulting from a drug overdose. In March 2026, investigators received information about an individual who was believed to have sold drugs to the deceased.
Officers began an extensive investigation and on Wednesday, April 22, 2026, we executed a search warrant on a residence located in the 2500 block of 49 Avenue S.E. During the search, officers seized approximately 151.9 grams of hydromorphone, 7.5 grams of methamphetamine and 6.6 grams of fentanyl. A stolen e-bike worth over $9,000 was also recovered from the residence, which has since been returned to its rightful owner. One man was arrested from the scene.
As a result, Jesse KABATOFF, 44, of Calgary, was charged with:
Four counts of drug trafficking
Five counts of possession of property obtained by crime
Three counts of possession of a controlled substance for the purpose of trafficking
Breach of a release order
KABATOFF will next appear in court on Tuesday, June 2, 2026.
The investigation remains ongoing and additional stolen property and drug-related charges are pending.
While there is insufficient evidence to lay charges in relation to the overdose death, the evidence found during the investigation identified drug traffickers who had previous contact with the deceased, which led our officers to conduct this investigation.
About overdose investigations
In 2023, the CPS began an OverDOSE pilot project, which utilized drug investigators to maintain consistency in all overdose or drug-related death investigations. In 2025, the Service formalized this pilot project with the creation of the Drug Investigations for Safer Communities (DISC) Team.
The DISC Team targets drug traffickers responsible for selling substances causing overdose deaths, as well as the sale of illicit drugs online. The DISC Team works closely with our existing Drug Undercover Street Team (DUST) to conduct investigations.

title: Calgary police make additional arrests in extortion series
content:
Our Organized Crime & Offender Management Section has laid charges against five men in relation to an elaborate scheme to extort a Calgary victim. This incident is an escalation of the ongoing violence associated to an extortion series that is targeting the South Asian community.
Between 6:30 and 7:30 p.m., on Wednesday, May 6, 2026, a man was kidnapped from a residence in Edmonton by four men. The victim was assaulted, threatened at gunpoint and forced into a vehicle where he was driven from Edmonton to Calgary. The suspects drove the victim to his friend’s residence, located in the Calgary community of Cityscape, where additional suspects and vehicles were staged. The suspects attempted to force the victim to lure his friend, who was the intended extortion target, out of his residence. The victim refused and was assaulted again.
While the suspects remained outside of the Cityscape residence, the victim’s friend and his wife attempted to leave their residence. They were followed and threatened with a firearm before fleeing back to their residence where they contacted police.
Shortly after, police located a vehicle that was believed to be involved and conducted a traffic stop in the area near Metis Trail and 104 Avenue N.E. Two men were arrested during the traffic stop for their involvement in the Cityscape incident, however the initial Edmonton kidnapping remained unreported to police.
The remaining suspects drove the kidnapping victim back to Edmonton where he was released near his residence early the next morning. Upon his release, he contacted police to report the kidnapping.
On Tuesday, May 12, 2026, with the assistance of the CPS Tactical Team, investigators located two additional suspects and conducted a traffic stop in the 600 block of Martindale Boulevard N.E., where they were arrested.
Investigators seized a privately-manufactured firearm (PMF) as part of this investigation.

title: Coach charged with sexual assault
content:
We have charged a man with sexual assault, following an investigation into allegations brought forward last week.
On Tuesday, May 12, 2026, police received a report that a minor had been sexually abused over a period of nearly two years by a volunteer coach at Henry Wise Wood High School. It is believed the suspect met the victim when she was 15 and used his position of trust to groom the victim before the assaults began. The victim had contact with the suspect through her school, as well as through extracurricular activities.
Our Child Abuse Unit launched an investigation and arrested the suspect on Wednesday, May 13, 2026.

“Coaches are supposed to be instructors, leaders and mentors,” said Staff Sgt. Chris Tudor of the CPS Child Abuse Unit.  “Sexual exploitation by a person in a position of authority is a betrayal of those values, and offenders must be held accountable.”
The Calgary Police Service is proud to partner with Luna Child and Youth Advocacy Centre to support child abuse investigations, victims, and their families. Our Child Abuse Unit is located at Luna and is one of many organizations working collaboratively under one roof to provide a comprehensive, child-focused, and trauma-informed response to abuse. This integrated approach provides a safe space for children to receive support as they heal.

title: Police seek to identify man in relation to bank fraud scheme
content:
We are asking for the public’s help to identify a man believed to be involved in a bank fraud scheme operating in the city.
On Wednesday, May 20, 2026, at about 5:38 p.m., a victim received a phone call that appeared on caller ID as the Bank of Montreal (BMO). The caller identified themselves as a representative from the BMO fraud investigations department and told the victim her debit and credit cards had been compromised.
The caller had detailed personal information, including the victim’s full name, address and date of birth. Based on this information, the victim was persuaded to disclose her banking personal identification number (PIN), cut up her debit and credit cards and await a courier who would collect them.
At about 6 p.m., a grey Chevrolet SUV attended the victim’s residence and picked up the cards.
At about 8:15 p.m., the victim received a legitimate text alert from BMO security regarding a $12,500 transaction at the Ace Casino, located at 4040 Blackfoot Trail S.E. At that point, the victim realized she had been defrauded and contacted BMO to cancel her cards.
The suspect is described as a man in his mid

20s, about 5’9” tall, weighing roughly 160 pounds, with short black hair and a black beard. He was last seen wearing glasses, a long grey overcoat and black pants.

title: CTrain collision under investigation
content:
We are investigating a collision involving a CTrain and a pedestrian that occurred yesterday evening.
At approximately 5:20 p.m. on Thursday, May 21, 2026, a man in his 60s was walking southbound on Centre Street approaching Seventh Avenue S., when he entered the path of an oncoming train. The train was moving through the intersection on a green signal.

title: Researchers studying movement of submerged remains in the Bow River
content:
Leading researchers are partnering with the Calgary Police Service to conduct a scientific study designed to improve search and recovery efforts within the Bow River.
This collaborative project brings together expertise from the University of Saskatchewan and the University of Windsor in the fields of scavenging and aquatic ecology, with the goal of better understanding how submerged bodies move within the river environment.
As part of the study, pig cadavers will be equipped with GPS tracking devices that records depth, temperature and distance travelled, and placed in the Bow River. The cadavers will be monitored remotely, until they become buoyant, at which point trained personnel will conduct a controlled recovery. Pig cadavers are regularly used by researchers as a scientifically acceptable alternative for human bodies.
The information gathered during this study will be used to create predictive models that can inform future search strategies, including where and when human remains are most likely to be located in the Bow River.
“
“This research will give us data we simply don’t have right now. By understanding how remains behave in the Bow River – how far they travel, where they move and when they surface – we can develop evidence-based models that improve recovery efforts. Ultimately, this work is about helping investigators find answers sooner and bringing closure to families.”
Dr Iain Phillips, Adjunct Professor at the University of Saskatchewan
„
This study began several years ago in Saskatchewan, where findings directly contributed to the successful recovery of human remains in a homicide investigation.
The CPS remains committed to using evidence-based practices to support missing persons and homicide investigations. Sadly, it is a tragic reality that past missing persons have entered our city’s waterways and this is part of our efforts to ensure every available measure is taken to locate those who go missing.
Residents and Bow River users may notice activity related to the study over the coming weeks. We are asking all Calgarians not to approach, interfere with or disturb any research equipment.

title: Calgary Police Service Canine and Mounted Unit badge presentation
content:
The Calgary Police Service recognized the certification of four new Police Service Dog (PSD) teams, two Human Remains Detection Dog (HRDD) teams, four new Drug Detection Dog (DDD) teams from the Alberta Sheriffs and a newly certified Police Service Horse (PSH) team. A badge ceremony at the Calgary Police Canine Park celebrated these teams and their handlers for their achievement.
Badge recipients include:
PSD Ozzy: Sgt. Rob Tippett
PSD Pronto: Const. Keegan Metz
PSD Leroy: Const. Ryan Avery
PSD Zom: Const. Ryan Broder
HRDD Quinn: Const. Yannick Caron
HRDD Bill: Const. Daniel Rogers
DDD Lurko: Sgt. Jonathan Walker
DDD Scarlet: Sheriff Officer Yoosef Monib
DDD Seamus: Sheriff Officer Bar Sandrusi
DDD Portos: Sheriff Officer Kai Kleckner
PSH Archer: Const. Razvan Alexe
The Calgary Police Service Canine Unit has been supporting frontline policing for more than 66 years and continues to keep our communities safe. Canine teams provide specialized support in suspect apprehension, the detection of drugs and explosives, and the recovery of human remains. Breeds such as German shepherds, Belgian Malinois and Labrador retrievers are carefully chosen for their strong sense of smell, work ethic and ability to connect with handlers and the public. On average, each dog is deployed about four times per shift. All training is done in-house by experienced officers, who must first spend at least five years working patrol before joining the unit.
The Calgary Police Service has worked closely with the Alberta Sheriffs to develop drug detection capabilities within highway patrol operations, strengthening public safety partnerships across the province. This event also marks the 20th anniversary of the CPS Human Remains Detection Program and highlights the approximately 5,000 calls for service our canine teams respond to each year. The work was made possible through strong collaboration and contribution from the Alberta Sheriffs.
The Mounted Unit has a long history dating back to 1910. It has become a valued part of the Service. Mounted officers support a wide range of work, including patrol, public safety, search operations and ceremonial events. They also play a role in connecting with the community, drawing people in and creating positive, memorable interactions.
Today, Police Service Horses are named in honour of significant moments in Canadian military history. Each name serves as a tribute to the courage and sacrifice of those who have served our county.
The newest addition, PSH Archer, is a five-year-old Percheron and American Quarter Horse cross gelding. Despite being smaller than some others in the unit, Archer brings a strong work ethic, agility and a steady temperament to his role. Since beginning his training with the Calgary Police Service last year, he has quickly shown his capability across all aspects of mounted patrol work. Known for his friendly and playful personality, Archer is often the first to greet visitors and is always eager to get to work. Named after Operation ARCHER, his name honours Canada’s contributions to international efforts in Afghanistan, including supporting the development and training of Afghan security forces as part of a broader mission to rebuild national institutions.
The badge presentation recognized the certification of these teams, while celebrating the continued achievement and transition of service within the Calgary Police Service Canine and Mounted units. It also recognized the important role families and trainers play in supporting these teams behind the scenes.

title: Joint operation results in 42 warrants executed and nine arrested
content:
A two-day joint operation between the Calgary Police Service (CPS) and the Alberta Sheriffs has resulted in nine people being arrested on outstanding warrants.
On Wednesday, May 20, and Thursday, May 21, 2026, officers from District 3 partnered with members of the Alberta Sheriffs’ Fugitive Apprehension Sheriff Support Team (FASST) to target wanted individuals residing within District 3. Four teams, each comprised of Calgary police officers and Alberta sheriffs conducted investigations and executed warrants across the district.
Throughout the operation, officers checked 75 addresses and arrested nine individuals on outstanding warrants. In total, 42 warrants were executed, 30 of which were related to criminal offences such as:
·
Fraud under $5,000
·
Fail to comply with probation order
·
Fail to comply with release order
·
Fail to attend court
·
Fail to attend for fingerprinting
·
Possession of unpackaged tobacco
·
Possession of drugs for the purpose of trafficking
·
Break and enter
·
Mischief to property under $5,000
12 additional warrants were related to bylaw or traffic offences, which included:
·
Driving without insurance
·
Fishing without a licence
·
Trespassing
Officers also collected approximately $3,000 in outstanding fines.
“This operation demonstrates the effectiveness of co-ordinated enforcement efforts in holding offenders accountable and improving safety in our communities,” says CPS District 3 Insp. Kelly Chalmers. “Our partnership with the Alberta Sheriffs continues to strengthen our ability to locate wanted individuals and address ongoing concerns within the district.”
“Operations like this highlight the strength of our ongoing partnership with the Calgary Police Service,” says Darren Thompson, Inspector, Alberta Sheriffs. “By working side by side and combining our resources and expertise, we’re able to more effectively locate individuals with outstanding warrants and support safer communities. The Fugitive Apprehension Sheriff Support Team is proud to collaborate on initiatives like this that demonstrate the impact of co-ordinated enforcement.”
The Calgary Police Service extends its thanks for the Alberta Sheriffs for their continued collaboration and support.

title: Two more men charged in extortion series
content:
Our Organized Crime & Offender Management (OCOM) Section continues to make significant progress in investigating a series of extortions targeting the South Asian community. Two more men are now facing charges.
On Tuesday, May 5, 2026, a business located in the northeast community of Saddle Ridge received demands for money from an unknown person, who claimed the business owner, and their friends and family members would be shot if the victim did not comply. The victim contacted police, and members of our OCOM Section immediately began investigating.
On Thursday, May 28, 2026, investigators took four men into custody while officers also executed a search warrant on a residence located in the 0 to 100 block of Saddlestone Way N.E.
Two of the men have now been charged.
Sandeep SINGH, 30, and Amandeep MALLHI, 39, both of Calgary, are charged with one count each of extortion and possession of proceeds of crime. They will next appear in court today, Monday, June 1, 2026.
“
We are making meaningful progress in this complex series of extortions, and these latest charges are a direct result of focused, coordinated and proactive police work. Each arrest reinforces our commitment to protecting Calgarians and sends a clear message to those involved in these crimes. Extortion will not be tolerated in our city and those responsible will be pursued, arrested and held accountable for their actions.
Supt. Jeff Bell of the CPS Criminal Operations & Intelligence Division
„
Since this series began in April 2025, there have been 46 extortion-related incidents in Calgary. Nineteen involved shootings at homes, businesses, or vehicles. To date, 14 individuals have been charged with 54 criminal offences in relation to the Calgary series.
The CPS continues to work closely with the Canada Border Services Agency and other law enforcement partners to address these incidents.
Anyone with information is asked to contact police by calling 403-266-1234. Tips can also be submitted anonymously to Crime Stoppers through any of the following methods:

We’d like to thank the community for their support and trust, which has been critical to progress these investigations.

, remains available for individuals to share information directly with investigators.
If you are the victim of extortion, do not pay and contact police immediately.

title: Three impaired driving collisions in three hours
content:
We are reminding Calgarians about the dangers of impaired driving, following three separate collisions on Wednesday, June 3, 2026.
The first occurred at around 7 p.m. along southbound Deerfoot Trail, between 24 Street and Barlow Trail S.E. Three vehicles were involved, including one that rolled onto its roof.  The driver believed to be responsible was treated for minor injuries and arrested for impaired driving.
We are asking anyone who may have dashcam footage of this incident to contact us.
The second collision happened just before 10 p.m. at the intersection of 88 Avenue and 52 Street N.E.
Once again, three vehicles were involved, one vehicle rolled, and one driver was arrested for suspected impaired driving.
The third collision occurred at 10:20 p.m. at Centre Street N and 56 Avenue N.E.  Several vehicles were involved and a driver was taken into custody, suspected of driving impaired.
As of the end of April 2026, our officers have issued 761 immediate roadside sanctions and laid 32 Criminal Code convictions for impaired driving.
Impairment is also believed to be a factor in one of the 11 fatal collisions in Calgary so far this year.  One fatal collision due to impaired driving is too many.
“The consequences of impaired driving can be devastating yet it is an entirely preventable crime,” says Staff Sgt. Rob Patterson with the CPS Traffic Section. “We are reminding Calgarians there is never an excuse to drive after drinking and encourage them to catch a ride with a friend, a taxi, or any rideshare option.
If you spot a driver who may be impaired, call 911.  Anyone with information about these incidents is asked to contact police by calling 403-266-1234. Tips can also be submitted anonymously to Crime Stoppers through any of the following methods:

title: Police investigate three shootings in 24-hour period
content:
Our District 3 General Investigations Unit is investigating three separate shootings that occurred in the communities of Forest Lawn, Beltline and East Village.
On Friday, June 5, 2026, at approximately 5:50 a.m., officers responded to reports of a man suffering from gunshot wounds after he approached a residence in the 1200 block of 44 Street S.E. seeking assistance.
It is believed the man was shot inside a residence in the 1400 block of 44 Street S.E. He was seen exiting the residence with a man and a woman.
The man and woman fled the scene prior to police arrival.
The victim was transported to the hospital in stable condition.
On Friday, June 5, 2026, at approximately 10:55 p.m., police responded to reports of a shooting at a residence in the 1300 block of 14 Avenue S.W.
Upon arrival, officers located a man suffering from gunshot wounds.
The victim was transported to hospital in critical condition and was later upgraded to stable; however, his injuries are considered life-altering.
While assessing the scene, investigators determined that a second victim had also sustained injuries consistent with gunshot wounds.
Witnesses reported that the second victim fled the scene prior to police arrival.
Based on evidence, it is believed the second victim left the area in a vehicle and was transported to the hospital.
The driver was taken into custody and remanded on warrants.
Upon arrival at the hospital, the second victims’ condition was determined to be stable with life-altering injuries.
At approximately 2:40 a.m., today, Saturday, June 6, 2026, police responded to reports of a man suffering from gunshot wounds underneath a bridge in the 400 block of 5 Avenue S.E.
It is believed the victim was approached by two unknown men who demanded his belongings.
A physical altercation ensued, during which one of the men shot the victim.
The men fled the scene while the victim was able to seek assistance.
He was transported to hospital in stable condition with minor injuries.
No suspects are in custody in relation to these investigations.
As these investigations are in the early stages, no further information is available at this time. Anyone who may have witnessed any of these incidents or has video from the area during the relevant times is asked to contact police.
“Following National Day Against Gun Violence, it’s concerning to see that firearm-related incidents continue to happen across our city, causing real harm and fear in our communities,” says Acting Staff Sergeant Corey Der. We remain committed to addressing gun violence and rely on the public to continue reporting information that helps keep everyone safe.”

Man charged in weekend shooting, police seek public assistance in identifying additional suspects
Investigators have arrested and charged one man in relation to a shooting that occurred on the weekend in the city’s northeast.
At approximately 3 a.m., yesterday, Sunday, June 7, 2026, police responded to reports of a man who had allegedly been shot outside of the Samosa House, located at 4310 104 Avenue N.E. It is believed the shooting occurred as a result of an altercation that took place outside of the Head Over Heels lounge, located in the same plaza.
The suspect was observed fleeing the scene in a grey 2019 Dodge Charger bearing Alberta license plate CTJ 0073 at a high rate of speed before police arrived. With the assistance from HAWCS, officers were able to safely conduct a traffic stop and took the suspect into custody. Police seized a loaded Canik TP9sfx 9mm handgun from the vehicle.
Though the suspect’s vehicle has been located, investigators are releasing a photo of the vehicle in hopes of advancing the investigation, in case people have information who have not yet come forward.
A photo of the seized firearm is also available on the City of Calgary Newsroom.
As a result, Gurshish Singh BRAR, 22, is charged with one count each of discharging a firearm with intent and unauthorized possession of a firearm. BRAR will appear in court today, Monday, June 8, 2026.
Police continue to look for two additional suspects who are believed to be involved in the shooting. Witnesses describe the suspects as men between 20 and 30 years old.
The victim, while shot at, was found to be uninjured. Police continue to investigate the circumstances that led to the incident.
